text: The surgeon identifies and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin, beginning dissection near the trite ligament. The vein is isolated and clipped. The mesocolon is detached from the pancreatic edge, and the torus fascia is divided from the gyrotus plane. The peritoneal layer is opened towards the pelvis to identify the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is then isolated. The sigmoidal artery is dissected and clipped at its origin, sparing the mesenteric artery.
The surgeon dissects the parietal peritoneum along the lateral margin of the colon, starting from the proximal sigmoid colon and extending to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a mesorectum dissection starting posteriorly, moving down into the pelvis, and then laterally to separate the rectal wings. An incision is made anteriorly in the peritoneum one centimeter from the Douglas reflection to remove the Douglas pouch. The distal rectum is separated using a robotic stapler. Endocyanin green fluorescence angiography is conducted to assess vascularization, repeated after the colorectal anastomosis.
